content.full_text:
December 6, 1994


Kathleen M. Weinheimer
City Attorney
City of Pismo Beach
1020 Calle Malaga
Santa Barbara, CA  93109

RE:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-94-365

Dear Ms. Weinheimer:

     This is in regard to your letter requesting advice with
respect to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1  The Commission does not provide third
party advice.  (Regulation 18329(b)(8).)  However, according to
our telephone conversation of October 18, 1994, you are requesting
advice about another person's duties under the Act based upon your
duty, as city attorney, to advise rather than upon specific
authorization.  (Regulation 18329(c)(1).)  Therefore, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.2

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.  In




_______________
     1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

     2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  ●  P.O. Box 807  ●  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  ●  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 2


addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.3 The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1.      Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
prevent Councilmember Paul Bailey from participating in City of
Pismo Beach decisions concerning the following:

     a.  Litigation against the City of Pismo Beach in which he
is a plaintiff; and

     b.  Decisions involving police levels of services, including
personnel decisions concerning the named police officers in the
lawsuit, and other decisions which may affect named defendants in
the lawsuit.

2.     May Councilmember Hal Halldin participate in any
governmental decisions which affect Councilmember Bailey and a
city consultant?

3.     If there is a conflict, may these public officials be
barred from participating in closed sessions?

CONCLUSIONS

1. a. and b.   Councilmember Bailey may not participate in
any city decisions concerning the lawsuit in which he is the
plaintiff, or any other decisions if such decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
councilmember's business or any of his economic interests.
Moreover, Councilmember Bailey may not participate in any decision
where the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of the councilmember, or his immediate
family increasing or decreasing by at least $250.

2.     If Councilmember Bailey and the city consultant are
sources of income of Councilmember Halldin, Councilmember Halldin
may not participate in any governmental decisions which will have
a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on
Councilmember Bailey or, the city consultant.


_________________________

3 Pursuant to our conversation on November 21, 1994, you
indicated that since you were not authorized to request this
advice, you could not provide us with additional information
pertaining to the officials' economic interests. As you are
aware, Commission written advice is the application of the law to
a particular set of facts provided by the requestor. (Regulation
1829(b)(7).) To the extent that the facts are not correct, our
advice may differ.

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 3




     3.   If there is a conflict, these public officials are
barred from participating in closed sessions where the matter for
which they are disqualified is discussed.

                                FACTS⁴

     As a result of the November 8, 1994, elections, Mr. Paul
Bailey and Mr. Hal Halldin have been elected to the City of Pismo
Beach City Council.

     Councilmember Bailey, his immediate family, and his business
are plaintiffs in a $10 million dollar federal civil rights action
against the City of Pismo Beach, certain outgoing city
councilmembers, the city's chief of police, and several named
police officers. As part of his suit, Councilmember Bailey
asserts that the defendants have increased police activity toward
his cocktail lounge and that this "targeting" by the police has
resulted in significant economic loss to his business.

     Councilmember Bailey has also filed, but not yet served on
the city, another lawsuit which alleges damages as a result of a
planning permit which he asserts was unfairly denied. The suit
names, in addition to the city, the city administrator, the
community development director, and the city attorney.
Councilmember Bailey has reported on his Form 721 various
investment interests, real property interests, and sources of
income.

     The second councilmember, Mr. Hal Halldin, has reported on
his Form 721 an ownership interest in a number of properties in
Pismo Beach and the surrounding area, including real property
jointly owned with councilmember Bailey. Councilmember Halldin
also has identified Councilmember Bailey in connection with
reportable "Income and Loan to a Business Entity or Trust" in
Schedule H-2 of his Form 721. Finally, Councilmember Halldin
holds a deed of trust on residential property owned by the primary
land use consultant in Pismo Beach.

     The city council is expected to make various governmental
decisions concerning the litigation in question and personnel
matters involving police officers named in Councilmember Bailey's
lawsuit.

                                ANALYSIS

     One of the purposes of the Political Reform Act is to ensure
that public officials perform their duties in an impartial manner,
free from bias caused by the own financial interests or the
financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section

_________________________
⁴ The facts contained in this letter are summarized from your
letter dated November 14, 1994, and our telephone conversations on
November 18, 1994, and November 21, 1994.

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 4


81001.) Councilmembers Bailey and Halldin are public officials
under the Act. (Section 82048.)

A.     Economic Interests

     In furtherance of this goal, the Act prohibits a public
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision
in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or any member of his or her immediate
family. (Section 87103.)

     Section 87103 provides in pertinent part:

          An official has a financial interest in a
     decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
     is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
     have a material financial effect, distinguishable
     from its effect on the public generally, on the
     official or a member of his or her immediate family
     or on:

          (a) Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

          (b) Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

          (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
     and other than loans by a commercial lending
     institution in the regular course of business on
     terms available to the public without regard to
     official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
     received by or promised to the public official
     within 12 months prior to the time when the
     decision is made.

          (d) Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.

                    *    *    *    *

          For purposes of this section, indirect
     investment or interest means any investment or
     interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
     a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
     public official, or by a business entity or trust

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 5


in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 87103(a) - (d).

Councilmember Bailey: You stated that Councilmember Bailey
has a number of economic interests which may be affected by city
decisions. Presumably, the councilmember has an interest in real
property in an amount greater than $1,000. (Section 87103(b).)
In addition, it appears that the councilmember has an interest in
a business entity for purposes of Sections 87103(a) and (d), with
various sources of income within the meaning of Section 87103(c).
Thus, Councilmember Bailey is prohibited from making or in any way
participating in decisions which will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on these economic
interests.

Councilmember Halldin: It appears that Councilmember Halldin
also has real property interests which could be affected by city
council decisions. It appears he also receives income from
various sources, presumably in an amount aggregating more than
$250 in a calendar year. Therefore, he is prohibited from making
or in any way participating in decisions which would have a
foreseeable material financial effect on these economic interests.

B.     Foreseeability and Materiality

The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey
Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

The Commission has adopted several regulations which define
material financial effect. Regulation 18702 sets forth the
general guidelines for determining whether an official's financial
interest in a decision is "material" as required by Section 87103.
If the official's financial interest is directly involved in the
decision, Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality. If
the official's financial interest is indirectly affected by the
decision, Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6 apply to determine
whether the effect of the decision is material.

1.     Councilmember Bailey

The primary question you pose is whether Councilmember Bailey
may participate in City of Pismo Beach decisions concerning
litigation against the City of Pismo Beach in which he is a
plaintiff. You have indicated that a major financial effect of

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 6


any decision regarding the lawsuit filed by Councilmember Bailey
would be whether the city is liable for the councilmember's
economic loss of business.  You also indicate that the
councilmember's potential participation in the litigation could
include decisions concerning settlement of legal claims and
strategic choices involving the city and individually named
defendants.  Therefore, it appears reasonably foreseeable that
city decisions concerning the lawsuit would affect the
councilmember and his business.

     Councilmember Bailey's business is a named plaintiff in the
lawsuit.  Thus, it is deemed to be "directly involved" in any city
decision affecting the lawsuit.  (Regulation 18702.1, copy
enclosed.)  Therefore, Councilmember Bailey may not participate in
any litigation decisions which will have any financial effect on
his business.  Of course, the determination of materiality is
necessarily a factual determination that must be made with respect
to each specific decision.  The Commission does not act as the
finder of fact.  (In re Oglesby, supra.)  We must leave this
determination to you and the city councilmember.

     In addition, the effect of a decision on a public official is
deemed to be material if the official is directly involved in the
decision and the decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets or liabilities of the official or his immediate
family increasing or decreasing by at least $250.  (Regulation
18702.1(a)(4).)  Thus, for example, if a decision made by the
councilmember could result in increasing or decreasing his
liability for litigation costs by $250, it would be considered a
material financial effect.  The councilmember would then be
precluded from participating in any such decision.

     You also ask whether Councilmember Bailey may participate in
city decisions involving police levels of services and personnel
matters of police officers named in the lawsuit.  While these
decisions may involve named defendants in Councilmember Bailey's
lawsuit against the city, it appears that Councilmember Bailey's
financial interests would be indirectly involved in these city
decisions.  Thus, it seems that the appropriate regulations to
apply concerning governmental decisions which do not concern the
litigation in question, but which may indirectly affect
Councilmember Bailey's economic interests, are Regulations
18702.2 - 18702.6 (copies enclosed).

_____________________
     5  You have not indicated that the councilmember has any economic
interests in any specific officers.

     6  Please note, however, that sometimes decisions are too
interlinked to be considered separately.  Thus, for example, if a
particular decision would alter a decision for which the official
has a conflict of interest, the official may be disqualified as to
both decisions.  (Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038.)

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 7


2.  Councilmember Halldin

You also ask whether Councilmember Halldin may participate in
the above decisions and other decisions involving a city
consultant.

On the basis of the facts you have provided, it appears that
Councilmember Halldin has various potentially disqualifying
economic interests, including real property interests and sources
of income.  While we have insufficient information to determine
all of the economic interests of the councilmember, it appears
that Councilmember Bailey and the city consultant are sources of
income to Councilmember Halldin.  In addition, it also appears
that Councilmember Halldin has a real property interest in
property owned by the city consultant.7  Therefore, if this is the
case, Councilmember Halldin may not participate in decisions
affecting either Councilmember Bailey or the city consultant,
where the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on them.

If Councilmember Halldin's financial interests are directly
before the city, the appropriate standard to apply is Regulation
18702.1.  If Councilmember Halldin's financial interests are
indirectly involved in the decision, the appropriate standards to
apply are included in Regulations 18702.2 - 18702.6.  You have not
provided sufficient facts for us to decide which regulation
applies.

C.   Making, Participating in Making or Attempting to Influence a
     Governmental Decision

A public official makes a governmental decision whenever the
official votes on a matter, commits the city to a course of
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the
city.  (Regulation 18700 (b).)  In addition, a public official
participates in a governmental decision when, acting within the
authority of their position, he or she negotiates, or advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker.  (Regulation
18700(c).)  For example, Councilmember Bailey would be making a
governmental decision if he votes on a matter concerning the
litigation, or commits his agency to a particular course of action
regarding the lawsuit.

In addition, under Regulation 18700.1(a), a public official
uses his or her official position to influence a decision if
for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency.

_____________________
7 The definition of an "interest in real property" includes a
deed of trust held on property as security on a note.  (Section
82033.)  Presumably, Councilmember Halldin's interest in the
consultant's property is greater than $1,000.

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 8


Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances
or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity,
client, or customer.

If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a
material financial effect the councilmembers' business interests,
real property, or sources of income, they must disqualify
themselves from making or participating in making the decision as
discussed above. They also may not use their official position to
influence the decision.

You specifically mentioned in our telephone conversation of
November 18, 1994, that the councilmembers may want to attend
closed sessions where they may be disqualified from a matter.
We have interpreted the case of Hamilton v. Town of Los Gatos
(1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1050 to preclude a disqualified
councilmember from attending a closed session of the city council.
(Armas Advice Letter, No. A-90-499a; Haile Advice Letter,
No. A-90-499b.) Therefore, an official may not attend a closed
session of the city council concerning a matter for which the
official is disqualified, and materials presented to the city
council for a closed session of the city council may not be
provided to the official when he or she is disqualified with
respect to that matter.

However, an official is not precluded from being present at
an open meeting of the city council. Except for confidential
material, material scheduled for discussion at an open session of
the city council need not be withheld from the official where he
or she is disqualified from participating in a city council
decision. (Armas Advice Letter, supra; Haile Advice Letter,
supra.)

D.    Participating in a Non-Official Capacity

Both Regulation 18700(d)(2) and Regulation 18700.1(b)(1)
(copies enclosed) allow a public official to appear, as any member
of the general public, before an agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function, to represent himself or herself
on matters related solely to his or her personal interests. A
"personal interest" includes an interest in real property or a
business entity which is wholly owned by the official or members
of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18700.1, subdivisions
(b)(1)(A) and (b)(1)(B).)

Once an official has formally disqualified himself or herself
by announcing the official's disqualification and the reason
therefor on the public record (Regulation 18700(b)(5)), the
official's comments should be made under the same procedures
applicable to comments from members of the public. For example,
the official is required to address the city council from the
audience. (Woodhead Advice Letter, No. A-90-768.)

Our File No. I-94-365
Page 9


Regulation 18700.1(b)(2) further allows a disqualified public
official to communicate with the general public or the press.

If you have any questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division


Enclosures
